subject: EU Law
lesson_title: The Architecture of EU Citizenship: Articles 20–21 TFEU and Directive 2004/38

questions.question: EU citizenship was formally introduced by which Treaty?

options.B: The Treaty of Maastricht 1992, which inserted what are now Articles 20–25 TFEU (originally Articles 17–22 TEC)

questions.explanation: EU citizenship was introduced by the Treaty of Maastricht (Treaty on European Union 1992), which created the concept and inserted citizenship provisions into the EC Treaty as Articles 17–22 TEC (now Articles 20–25 TFEU post-Lisbon). The Lisbon Treaty renumbered but did not fundamentally change these provisions. EU citizenship was a significant constitutional innovation — it established a direct legal relationship between the EU and its citizens, not mediated solely through member state nationality. The citizenship provisions build upon the earlier sectoral free movement rights but go further by establishing citizenship as a foundational status of EU law.

questions.question: Who is an EU citizen under Article 20(1) TFEU?

options.A: Any person who has resided in the EU for five years
options.B: Every person holding the nationality of a member state — EU citizenship is additional to and does not replace national citizenship; determination of nationality is exclusively a matter for each member state
options.C: Any person born in an EU member state
options.D: Any person with the right of permanent residence under Directive 2004/38

questions.explanation: Article 20(1) TFEU: 'Every person holding the nationality of a Member State shall be a citizen of the Union. Citizenship of the Union shall be additional to and not replace national citizenship.' Nationality is determined exclusively by each member state under its own constitutional rules — member states retain sovereign control over the grant of their nationality and thus over access to EU citizenship. The CJEU confirmed in Micheletti [1992] that member states cannot refuse to recognise EU citizenship granted by another member state. In Rottmann [2010], the Court held that EU law limits member states' power to withdraw nationality when this would result in loss of EU citizenship — EU law must be taken into account.

questions.question: Article 21 TFEU confers on every EU citizen the right to move and reside freely within the territory of the member states, subject to limitations and conditions laid down in the Treaties and secondary legislation. Is Article 21 directly effective?

options.A: No — Article 21 merely requires member states to legislate for free movement rights; it creates no directly enforceable rights
options.B: Yes — the CJEU confirmed in Baumbast and R [2002] that Article 21 (then Article 18 TEC) is directly effective and confers directly enforceable rights of residence on EU citizens, subject to limitations that must be applied proportionately
options.C: Yes, but only for EU citizens who are economically active
options.D: Yes, but Article 21 can only be invoked in national courts, not before the CJEU

questions.explanation: Baumbast and R [2002] confirmed the direct effect of Article 21 TFEU. Mr Baumbast was a German national who had worked in the UK but was no longer employed; he had comprehensive sickness insurance and sufficient resources. The UK argued he no longer had a right of residence under Directive 73/148. The CJEU held that Article 18 TEC (now Article 21 TFEU) confers a directly effective right of residence on EU citizens independently of the conditions in secondary legislation — subject to limitations that must themselves be applied proportionately. This was a constitutional breakthrough: citizenship itself, independently of economic activity, became a source of EU free movement rights.

questions.question: Directive 2004/38 consolidates and replaces earlier Directives and Regulations on free movement of persons. It provides for a tiered residence system. What are the three tiers?

options.A: Entry rights; three-month residence; five-year residence
options.B: A right of entry and residence for up to three months without conditions; a right of residence for more than three months subject to conditions (being a worker, self-employed, self-sufficient with comprehensive health insurance, or a student); and a permanent right of residence after five years of lawful continuous residence
options.C: Short-term residence (one month); medium-term residence (one year); long-term residence (five years)
options.D: Economic residence; educational residence; retirement residence

questions.explanation: Directive 2004/38 creates a three-tier system: (1) Tier 1 (Articles 6): Right of residence for up to three months — any EU citizen can reside in another member state for up to three months without conditions or formalities (beyond holding a valid ID or passport). (2) Tier 2 (Article 7): Right of residence for more than three months — requires being a worker/self-employed; having sufficient resources and comprehensive sickness insurance; being a student with sufficient resources and insurance; or a family member joining such a person. (3) Tier 3 (Article 16): Permanent residence — after five years of lawful continuous residence, the right of residence becomes unconditional and the five-year review conditions no longer apply.

questions.question: What rights does Article 20(2) TFEU guarantee to EU citizens?

options.A: Only the right to vote and stand in municipal elections in their member state of residence
options.B: The right to move and reside freely; the right to vote and stand in European Parliament and municipal elections in the member state of residence; the right to consular protection from other member states' diplomatic missions when in a third country where one's own member state has no representation; and the right to petition the European Parliament and complain to the European Ombudsman
options.C: Only economic rights — the social and political rights listed are non-binding aspirations
options.D: The same rights as nationals of the member state of residence in all respects

questions.explanation: Article 20(2) TFEU lists the rights attached to EU citizenship: (a) the right to move and reside freely within the EU (Article 21); (b) the right to vote and stand as a candidate in European Parliament elections in the member state of residence (Article 22); (c) the right to vote and stand in municipal elections in the member state of residence (Article 22); (d) the right to protection by the diplomatic or consular authorities of any other member state when in a third country where one's own member state is not represented (Article 23); (e) the right to petition the European Parliament and apply to the European Ombudsman (Article 24); (f) the right to write to EU institutions in any official EU language (Article 24). These rights are subject to the conditions in the Treaties and implementing measures.

questions.question: Directive 2004/38 Article 7(1) requires that EU citizens (other than workers and self-employed) exercising the right of residence for more than three months have 'sufficient resources'. What does this requirement mean in practice?

options.A: EU citizens must demonstrate income above the national minimum wage of the host state
options.B: EU citizens must have resources sufficient that they will not become a burden on the social assistance system of the host state — the Directive does not specify a fixed amount; it is assessed in light of the individual's personal situation taking account of their financial circumstances, family situation, and housing costs
options.C: EU citizens must have at least €10,000 in savings to qualify
options.D: The sufficient resources requirement does not apply to citizens of EU member states with bilateral social security agreements with the host state

questions.explanation: The 'sufficient resources' condition is deliberately flexible — Directive 2004/38 Recital 10 states that the host state must take into account the personal situation of the person concerned, not apply a fixed threshold automatically. The Directive explicitly prohibits member states from laying down a fixed minimum amount of resources. The assessment is individual and contextual — a person with a modest income may have sufficient resources if their overall circumstances are stable (existing housing, limited needs, family support). Critically, the question is whether the person will become an 'unreasonable burden' on the social assistance system — not whether they will ever use any state services. A 'real risk of becoming an unreasonable burden' test applies.

questions.question: The 'comprehensive sickness insurance' requirement in Directive 2004/38 Article 7(1)(b) for economically inactive EU citizens was considered in Moveras and Others. What is the significance of this requirement?

options.A: It means economically inactive EU citizens must take out private health insurance regardless of any existing health entitlements
options.B: The requirement serves to ensure that the host state's healthcare system is not unreasonably burdened — however, the CJEU has held that entitlement to healthcare under the host state's own system (e.g., through work history, European Health Insurance Card coverage) can satisfy this requirement where equivalent protection exists; the requirement must be assessed proportionately
options.C: It applies equally to workers and economically inactive citizens
options.D: It requires international private medical insurance from an EU-recognised insurer

questions.explanation: The comprehensive sickness insurance requirement for economically inactive EU citizens is designed to prevent the host state's healthcare system from being burdened by non-contributing residents. However, the CJEU has taken a pragmatic approach: where an EU citizen already has equivalent health coverage (e.g., through a European Health Insurance Card valid in the host state, or through entitlement to host state healthcare by virtue of prior contributions), this can satisfy the requirement. The requirement must be applied proportionately — it cannot be used as a pretext to exclude EU citizens who pose no realistic healthcare cost burden. Member states cannot apply the requirement so strictly as to make the Article 7 right illusory.

questions.question: Directive 2004/38 Article 16 grants permanent residence after five years of lawful continuous residence. What is 'lawful' residence for these purposes?

options.A: Any physical presence in the host member state, including unauthorised stays
options.B: Residence in compliance with the conditions of the Directive — for those exercising worker or self-employed status (Article 7(1)(a)) or as a self-sufficient person with comprehensive health insurance (Article 7(1)(b)); periods of residence under more limited rights (e.g., the three-month unconditional right) do not count toward the five years
options.C: Only residence formally registered with the national authorities
options.D: Any residence of more than three months, regardless of compliance with Article 7 conditions

questions.explanation: Lawful residence for the purposes of Article 16 means residence in compliance with the Directive's conditions — specifically, residence meeting the Article 7 conditions (worker/self-employed/self-sufficient/student) or residence during which the person retained worker status under Article 7(3). The three-month unconditional right under Article 6 does not constitute 'lawful residence' for the five-year count. In Dias [2011], the CJEU confirmed that periods of residence that did not comply with Article 7 conditions (e.g., periods of residing without sufficient resources or insurance) do not count. A continuous period of qualifying lawful residence is required — significant absences (more than six consecutive months, or over six months per year in total for other reasons) break continuity.

questions.question: What are the consequences of acquiring permanent residence under Directive 2004/38?

options.A: Permanent residence holders become full nationals of the host member state
options.B: Permanent residents no longer need to satisfy the Article 7 conditions — they cannot be required to have sufficient resources or comprehensive health insurance; they have enhanced protection against expulsion (only on serious public policy/security grounds); and they enjoy equal treatment with nationals except in areas expressly excluded
options.C: Permanent residence is equivalent to citizenship of the host state for all purposes
options.D: Permanent residents automatically acquire a right to work in all other EU member states without further formalities

questions.explanation: Article 16 permanent residence removes the conditional nature of the Article 7 right: the holder is no longer required to demonstrate worker status, sufficient resources, or comprehensive sickness insurance. They enjoy equal treatment with nationals of the host state (Article 24 Directive 2004/38) in the full range of social and economic benefits — the distinction between economically active and inactive citizens which limits social benefits for Article 7 residents disappears. They are protected against expulsion only on 'serious grounds of public policy or public security' (Article 28(2)) — a higher threshold than for non-permanent residents. Permanent residence can be lost only by absences of more than two consecutive years from the host member state.

questions.question: Directive 2004/38 provides that EU citizens may be expelled from the host member state in certain circumstances. Article 14(3) states that an expulsion measure shall not be the 'automatic consequence' of what?

options.A: A failure to register with the national authorities within three months of arrival
options.B: Recourse to the social assistance system of the host member state — EU citizens cannot automatically be expelled simply because they have claimed social assistance; a holistic assessment must be made of whether they are an unreasonable burden
options.C: Being unemployed for more than six months

questions.explanation: Directive 2004/38 Article 14(3) expressly states: 'An expulsion measure shall not be the automatic consequence of an Union citizen's or his or her family member's recourse to the social assistance system of the host Member State.' This prevents automatic expulsion of EU citizens who claim benefits. The host state must carry out an individual examination of whether the person has become an 'unreasonable burden' on the social assistance system — taking into account the duration of residence, personal circumstances, the amount of benefit claimed, and whether the situation is temporary or permanent. Article 14(3) reflects the proportionality principle: being temporarily in need does not automatically justify expulsion.
